contents: Detection of High-Risk Histologic Features and TumorUpstaging of Nonmelanoma Skin Cancers on DebulkAnalysis: A Quantitative Systematic ReviewBabu Singh, MD,* Adriana Dorelles, ScD, MS,+ Nellie Konnikov, MD,++and Bichchau M. Nguyen, MD, MPH++xBACKGROUNDHistologic analysis of tumor debulks from Mohs micrographic surgery (MMS) or wide localexcision may lead to the detection of adverse features missed on initial biopsy.OBJECTIVEDetermine the incidence of (1) high-risk features on debulk analysis compared with initial biopsyand (2) upstaging of tumors on debulk analysis according to the American Joint Committee of Cancer-7thEdition (AJCC-7) and the Brigham and Women's Alternative (BWH) staging criteria.MATERIALS AND METHODSA comprehensive search strategy using PubMed/MEDLINE, Web of Science,and EMBASE was conducted to identify articles published from 1960 to present that detail histology of initialbiopsy and debulked tumor.RESULTSFourteen studies, encompassing 2,565 cases of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cellcarcinoma (SCC) were included in the data extraction process. BCCs (30.9%) were reclassified from a low-riskhistologic subtype to a high-risk subtype on debulk analysis (p < .001). Cases with perineural invasion (89.4%)were detected on debulk analysis. SCC tumors (9.1% and 11.1%) were upstaged according to the AJCC-7 andBWH Alternative criteria, respectively. Ninety percent of high-risk BWH T2b SCCs were inaccurately stagedlower on initial biopsy.CONCLUSIONTumor debulk analysis in MMS may aid in the identification of additional high-risk features,thereby improving staging accuracy, treatment decisions and patient outcomes.

The authors have indicated no significant interest with commercial supporters.Adverse histologic features (i.e., aggressivehistologic subtype, invasion into fat or muscle,perineural invasion [PNI], lymphovascular invasion[LVI]) directly affect staging, treatment, and outcomesfor patients with nonmelanoma skin cancers (NMSCs).Under the current American Joint Committee ofCancer-7th edition (AJCC-7) staging system for basalcell carcinoma (BCC) and squamous cell carcinoma(SCC), aggressive histologic subtype, invasion to fat ordeeper, and PNI can upstage a tumor from T1 to T2.1Tumors with adverse features may require aggressivesurgical treatment including Mohs micrographicsurgery (MMS) and potentially, imaging studies andadjuvant radiation, to reduce risk.2,3 Even withappropriate treatment, tumors with one or moreadverse histologic features have an increased risk oflocal recurrence, metastasis and disease-specific death.4Initial biopsy of a NMSC may fail to identify alladverse histologic features because of sampling error.Multiple studies have reported detection of aggressivehistologic subtype and PNI in specimens from widelocal excision (WLE) and MMS tumor debulk.5-18Clinical management based solely on findings frombiopsy may lead to the under treatment of high-risktumors and subsequent poor outcomes.*Department of Dermatology, Boston University Medical Center, Boston, Massachusetts; +Department of Economics,Arizona State University, Tempe, Arizona; ++Department of Dermatology, VA Boston Healthcare System, Jamaica PlainsCampus, Boston, Massachusetts; xDepartment of Dermatology, Tufts Medical Center, Boston, Massachusetts(c) 2017 by the American Society for Dermatologic Surgery, Inc. Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. All rights reserved.ISSN: 1076-0512* Dermatol Surg 2017;0:1-9* DOI: 10.1097/DSS.0000000000001146

With WLE, the residual tumor and marginal tissue areexamined en-bloc, allowing for the detection of addi-tional adverse features in the residual tumor. How-ever, with MMS, the central tumor is often discardedor curetted to define the clinical border before clear-ance of margins. Therefore, only the marginal surfaceis examined with MMS. Thus, there is a theoreticalrisk of under-staging because of incomplete histologicevaluation of the central tumor.MethodsSearch StrategyA comprehensive search of PubMed/MEDLINE, Webof Science, and EMBASE with terms for SCC, BCC,MMS, WLE, biopsy, and all available synonyms wasperformed to identify all relevant studies in the Englishlanguage published from 1960 to date.Study and Case Inclusion CriteriaSelect articles, including their references, were assessedin full for inclusion in the quantitative analysis. Articlesthat provided histologic data on both initial biopsy andsubsequent tumor debulk from WLE or MMS of BCC,SCC in situ, and SCC tumors were included. Studieswith less than 2 cases of NMSCs were excluded.Case Inclusion CriteriaCase were included only when both histologic featuresof the initial biopsy and debulk specimen were avail-able, even those with actinic keratosis or no residualtumor on the debulk analysis. Cases were excludedwhen incidental nonkeratinocytic tumors, for exam-ple, Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans or Micro-cystic Adnexal Carcinoma, were identified or thosethat were designated as "miscellaneous."

Data ExtractionExtracted variables from the selected studies includedlead author, year published, study design, number ofcases, consecutive nature of cases (i.e., if consecutivereferrals for MMS surgery were included, or if caseswere at random), definitive treatment modality(MMS or WLE) and major findings. Tumor charac-teristics extracted included tumor type, location, size(longest reported diameter), primary or recurrenthistologic subtype of the initial biopsy and debulkspecimen, presence of PNI or LVI, Clark level, anddepth of invasion (in millimeters). If more than onesubtype was reported, the more aggressive subtypewas recorded. If a single patient had more than oneNMSC, each tumor was included as a separate case inthe quantitative analysis. The designation SCC orBCC not otherwise specified (NOS) indicates thata specific histologic subtype was not reported, but theauthors of the article designated it as low-risk. Like-wise, Aggressive BCC or SCC designation indicatesthat the tumor was not specified but of a high-riskhistologic subtype.Tumor Staging

Squamous cell carcinoma tumor (T) stage based onAJCC-7 and Brigham and Women's Alternative(BWH) Alternative staging criteria was determinedusing histologic features of the initial biopsy anddebulked tumor. If the T stage on the debulk analysiswas lower than that of the initial biopsy, then the Tstage of the debulk specimen was designated the sameas the initial biopsy. High-risk features according tothe AJCC-7 include depth/invasion (>2-mm thickness,Clark level greater than or equal to IV, or PNI), ana-tomic location (ear or non-hair-bearing lip), and dif-ferentiation (poorly differentiated or undifferentiated).1AJCC T1 tumors are less than or equal to 2 cm ingreatest dimension with less than 2 high-risk featuresand T2 tumors are greater than or equal to 2 cm or anysize with greater than or equal to 2 high-risk features.Stage AJCC T3 and T4 tumors are those that invadebone or deeper structures. High-risk features accordingto the BWH Alternative system include tumor diametergreater than or equal to 2 cm, poorly differentiatedhistology, PNI greater than or equal to 0.1 mm, ortumor invasion beyond fat.19 BWH stage T1, T2a, T2b,T3 have 0, 1, 2 to 3, or greater than or equal to 4 high-risk factors, respectively. Tumor invading bone auto-matically upgrades tumors to BWH stage T2.Statistical AnalysisSummary statistics were presented as mean, inter-quartile range, or frequency count with percentage.

(c) 2017 by the American Society for Dermatologic Surgery, Inc. Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.Associations in histologic findings between initialbiopsy and subsequent debulk specimen wereaccessed using Pearson chi-squared test or Fisherexact test. Fisher exact test was used to computestatistical significance differences between tumorstage of SSC on initial biopsy and tumor stage afterdebulk analysis. Results with p values < .05 wereconsidered statistically significant. Statistical analy-sis was performed using STATA/IC (v.14, CollegeStation, TX).ResultsStudy CharacteristicsPatient and Tumor Demographicsreported sex. Tumors were primary (94.9%) and45.1% were located in the head and neck region. Atotal of 2,565 cases of BCC (2,079, 81%) and SCC(486, 19%) were collated. Although 73.7% had noreported size, 9.2% of the tumors had a diameter ofgreater than or equal to 2 cm.Initial Biopsy ResultsInitial diagnosis was made by punch biopsy in 52% ofcases, shave biopsy in 12%, and an unspecified biopsytechnique in 36% of cases. Majority of the BCC tumorswere reported as nodular on initial biopsy (40.3%).Fivehundred-nine(24.5%)casesofBCCwereclassifiedas high-risk (aggressive BCC NOS, basosquamous,infiltrative, micronodular, and morpheaform), and1,570 (75.5%) were classified as low-risk (superficial,nodular, and NOS). Of 486 cases of SCC, 4 (0.8%)were classified as high-risk (poorly differentiated andaggressive SCC NOS) and 482 (99.2%) were classifiedas low-risk (in situ, well-differentiated, moderatelydifferentiated, and NOS).

Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited.debulk analysis (p < .001). Of 47 cases with PNI, only3 (6.4%) were detected on initial biopsy and 42(89.4%) were detected in the debulk specimen. Therewas only one case of LVI, and it was detected in thedebulk specimen.Tumor Upstaging on Debulk AnalysisDiscussionThe authors' study is the only quantitative systematicreview to date that evaluates the incidence of adversehistologic features and tumor upstaging on debulkanalysis compared with initial biopsy specimens.Analysis of over 2,000 BCCs revealed a 30% incidenceof reclassification from low-risk to high-risk histologicsubtype for example, infiltrative, basosquamous,micronodular, and morpheaform. High-risk BCCsshould be excised with wider margins (4-6 mm) or withMMS.2,3 Treatment of these aggressive tumors aslow-risk BCCs with curettage, cryotherapy, or narrowmargin excision will likely result in higher rates of localrecurrence.20-22 Basal cell carcinomas account fornearly 80% of all skin cancers diagnosed in the UnitedStates, that is 2 million cases occur annually per year.23

Misclassification of high-risk BCCs as low-risk inTABLE 4. Tumor upstaging for SCC based on debulk analysiscompared with initial biopsy was 9.1% per AJCC-7and 11.1% per BWH Alternative staging criteria.Importantly, 90% (18/20) of BWH high-risk T2bSCCs were inaccurately staged as lower T stages. Intheir study proposing the BWH alternative stagingcriteria, Jambusaria-Pahlajani and colleagues19showed that BWH T2b SCCs have 43% risk oflocal recurrence, 72% nodal metastasis, and 83%SCC-related deaths. Although no guidelines currentlyexist, there is a suggested role of sentinel lymph nodebiopsy and adjuvant radiation therapy for BWH T2bSCCs. Furthermore, biopsy specimens failed to detect89% of cases of PNI. The National ComprehensiveCancer Network (NCCN) guidelines for localized,high-risk cutaneous SCC recommend adjuvant radia-tion therapy for cases with extensive PNI or large-nerve invasion.3 Debulk analysis can thus help identifyhigh-risk tumors that may require closer surveillance,nodal workup, and multidisciplinary care.

The results of the authors' study depended oncompleteness and accuracy of the articles includedin the quantitative systematic review. More than 70%of cases had missing tumor size. Diameter of nervesaffected by PNI was not reported. Squamous cell car-cinomas with missing data are thus likely under-stagedbased on both AJCC and BWH criteria which usetumor size and diameter of nerve invasion as stagingcriteria.Sixout of 14studies hadnonconsecutivecases,where the surgeons selected the tumors to be sent fordebulk analysis. These studies may have a higherincidence of adverse features because of selection biasas surgeons are presumably more likely to send clini-cally high-risk tumors for further analysis. Despitethese limitations, the data from the authors' study stilldemonstrate statistically significant reclassificationfrom low-risk to high-risk for 30% of BCCs, andupstaging to higher tumor T stage for approximately10% of SCCs. The authors' reported incidence of SCCupstaging on debulk analysis issimilarto thatobservedwith melanoma tumors. Studies have reported an 8%to 10% incidence of melanoma upstaging afterhistologic analysis of residual tumor from WLE.24,25

The authors' study highlights the pitfalls of relyingsolely on biopsy pathology results to dictate treatmentfor NMSCs. Thorough physical examination is crucialto determine extent of soft tissue spread, potentialnerve invasion signified by pain, numbness or muscleparalysis, and lymphatic spread. This is especially truefor lesions undergoing treatment with MMS. Thedecision to send tumor debulk for further analysisremains at the discretion of the Mohs surgeon, as thereis no applicable guidelines or consensus available.Further studies are needed to identify patient andtumor risk factors that correlate with an increasedincidence of high-risk histologic features observed ondebulk analysis. Once these patient and tumor riskfactors are established, guidelines may be formed forthe appropriate use of debulk analysis in MMS for thetreatment of NMSCs.ConclusionHistologic analysis of debulked tumor improves thedetection of high-risk features and accuracy of tumor(T) staging compared with initial biopsy. Debulkanalysismaythereforehelptooptimizemanagementforhigh-risk skin cancers and improve patient outcomes.References1. Edge SB, Byrd DR, Compton CC, Fritz AG, et al.
